data.title: EssilorLuxottica: Solid 2024 But Too Pricey
data.description:
On 12/02/2025, EssilorLuxottica Société Anonyme (OTCPK:ESLOF)(OTCPK:ESLOY) released its Q4 and FY 2024 results. Since our last update, Valuation Is Too High, Neutral Rating Confirmed, EssilorLuxottica increased by 24.27% (Fig 1). Our neutral rating was backed by 1) limited earnings per share growth in 2025 and 2026 with no justification for a 30x P/E multiple, 2) EssilorLuxottica's valuation was impacted by Meta equity stake interest and positive news flow on the sell side, and 3) no margin of error until the 2026 forecast. In a compelling market environment, we prefer to remain on the sidelines and continue to see risks skewed on the downside.

Fig 1

Q4 Results

The company reported Q4 total top-line sales of €6.78 billion, higher-than-expected organic growth of +5.6% year over year, and an M&A contribution of around €225 million. By region, the main beat was in the EU, with sales up 9.6%; APAC recorded a plus 14% year-on-year thanks to the consolidation of Stellest and Supreme. As anticipated, there was a sound recovery in North America, where Sunglass Hut grew high-single-digit. Q4 total sales were 1% above consensus. Going down to the P&L analysis, the company's FY24 adjusted EBIT reached €4.4 billion with a plus 5.7% year-on-year and adjusted net profit of €3.1 billion (Fig 2). H2's core operating profit was 2% below consensus, with a margin of 15.0%. In detail, we believe new strategic investments eroded 60 basis points of EBIT margin, and FX impacted an additional 30 basis points of EssilorLuxottica marginality. There was no change in the distribution policy, and the group proposed a dividend in line with last year at €3.95 per share.

Source: EssilorLuxottica Q4 results presentation - Fig 2

Why are we still neutral?

  1. Most sales are generated in mature markets, which are at risk due to increasing unemployment rates and weakening macro consumption. Additionally, any changes impacting private healthcare insurance subsidies (regarding ophthalmic lenses) could impact the company's sales. North America represents 46% of the company's total sales, and we believe unemployment rates will likely increase due to persistent inflation. With Trump's second mandate, there might be a lower Budget for Medicare Funding.
  2. Still related to the challenging operating environment, EssilorLuxottica planned to avoid US tariffs on imports from Mexico and China through production flexibility and commercial actions. This will require additional shipping costs or new CAPEX investment, which were not communicated in the earnings calls;
  3. Here at the Lab, we supported the company before moving EssilorLuxottica to a neutral rating. The Group was the Perfect Combination Of Tech, Pharma, And Fashion. Indeed, there are several new growth initiatives underway: 1) smart glasses (Ray-Ban & Meta), 2) Nuance Audio, and 3) myopia management with a focus on diagnostics/surgery. This new initiative will begin to drive sales, but limited disclosure exists. We increased our case assumption on smart glasses with volume growth of the high-single-digit. As a reminder, the company launched the second generation of Ray-Ban | Meta smart glasses in October 2023, and after a significant success by management teams, Meta and EssilorLuxottica announced a long-term partnership in September 2024. EssilorLuxottica is now expanding the production capacity for Ray-Ban Meta to reach 10 million annual units. Since September 2023, the group has sold more than 2 million units.
  4. As expected, Q4 showed a strong sequential acceleration (benefitting from M&A contribution), but the company needs a fundamental change in volume pace in 2025. Additionally, the company reiterated its 19/20% EBIT margin in 2026.

Earnings Changes and Valuation

Following the 9M results, we expect 2024 sales and core operating profit of €26.6 and €4.55 billion, respectively. We proved to be correct on the numbers. Considering the latest indication, we increase our Fiscal Year 2025 sales estimate by 1.5%, assuming a positive forex effect of 1.1%, and we revise the assumption of sales growth from 7.2% to 7.5%. So, our top-line sales reached almost €28.5 billion, and with a 19% EBIT margin (which represents 240 basis points marginality higher than 2024), we arrived at €5.4 billion. Even applying this high EBIT margin, our net income reached €3.9 billion, with an EPS of €8.42. The company's shares trade at a 34.6x 2025e P/E. Once again, we see a high valuation with no margin of error. Applying a 28x P/E target (Fig 3), our share price is set at €232 per share. This P/E corresponds to an average trading multiple in the 2021-2024. To justify a neutral rating, we should apply a 2026E P/E of 32x with an EBIT step-up and an improving outlook of long-term growth prospects for Nuance Audio and Stellest.

Fig 3

Risks

Our team has a long history of covering the company. We report our previous risks section: 1) consumer slowdown and high-price strategy might lead to lower top-line sales, 2) Chinese and US tariffs, 3) loss of valuable licensed brands due to higher competition, 4) value disruptive M&A, 5) FX changes and headwinds as happened in 2024 (the company reported in € terms that approximately 60% of the total company's turnover is generated outside Europe), 6) failure of new product launches, 7) the company is progressing toward a Pharma player, which exposed EssilorLuxottica to regulatory approvals (Stellest) or commercial rollout for new growth drivers. Additionally, we find it hard to value the company at this price. Any sales/earnings miss might significantly impact the company's share price.

Conclusion

We were expecting a season sales acceleration in Q4, but we continue to see EssilorLuxottica fully priced in. Even considering an EBIT step-up (hard to justify) with the ongoing challenges, this is not the right price to enter.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: YMAX: Bundling Ultra Strategies Has Worked Surprisingly Well
data.description:
Introduction

Regular readers of mine may remember when I initially covered the YieldMax Universe Fund of Option Income ETFs (NYSEARCA:YMAX) back in September last year. It was a turning point in on my ongoing coverage of YieldMax ETFs, where I had only ever given hold, sell, and even a strong sell rating to their ETFs. However, I gave a buy rating to YMAX, and have since had a different view of their funds.

YMAX is a fund-of-funds, meaning that it only owns other ETFs as its holdings. It holds strategies from across the YieldMax funds, effectively allowing investors to hold a basket of the firm's funds instead of buying them individually.

This bundling of strategies has added a layer of diversity to YMAX's returns, and has enabled it to perform very well against equity indexes and other popular equity-income funds.

I picked JEPI and JEPQ here because they have the highest AUM in the derivative equity funds category, but they are notably more conservative in strategy than YMAX.

This has drawn a lot of interest to YMAX, especially because the fund's positive returns are entirely made of income generated by the underlying strategies. Its distribution rate is up to 47.25%.

We can see evidence that the fund's returns are entirely from its yield because the fund's price is actually down from its market debut. The chart above is only showing total returns, which includes dividends paid, but not reinvested.

Note the gap between price and total return in this chart.

There are many income investors who are okay with this deal, where their capital may (read as: is likely to) suffer despite a positive total return via income. This is due to the need for high income outweighing the need for liquidity of capital, e.g., folks who are okay with tying up their shares so long as the income flows. There is also a group of investors who are re-investors, and habitually DRIP these kinds of instruments to accumulate shares over time and offset the capital losses that way.

Regardless of the justification for owning YMAX, what matters is that it has delivered what it promised, and done it well. Since my first recommendation, it certainly has. The timing was right, and the fund has dramatically outperformed in total return over the S&P 500.

YMAX Holdings

The actual holdings themselves look like this:

The fund owns twenty-nine other YieldMax funds, and holds them all at equal weights, net their price drift since the last monthly rebalance.

There are more funds in the YieldMax universe that YMAX does not own yet, and may buy into in the future. We can expect that as the catalog of funds YieldMax operates continues to expand, so will YMAX's holdings. Currently, I believe that YMAX is only missing the Carvana-linked ETF, CVNY.

This is a positive for the fund, because it means that it will continue to expand and diversify its exposure over time, potentially reducing its overall volatility further.

YMAX Risks

There are several major risks to consider when looking at these kinds of income vehicles, namely fund-of-funds like YMAX.

Risks of the Underlying Strategy

In my past articles on the single-stock funds like NVDY, where I take a deeper look at their options strategy, I have been somewhat pessimistic about the YieldMax plug-n-play strategy they use in their funds.

The strategy, which is employed by basically all the underlying funds in YMAX, goes like this:

  1. The fund buys T-Bills with its NAV.
  2. The fund then sells a put and buys a call on the underlying stock, creating a "synthetic long future" position, which effectively mimics the risk and reward structure of long stock.
  3. The fund then buys and sells call options daily against this synthetic long position.

This strategy ensures that the fund is maximizing its yield-earning potential, and participates in some upside of the stock. Typically, when looking at these funds, I see a lot of risk in having to trust the manager to pick the correct strikes, and to be on top of trends in the stock. Being wrong is what leads to that price decline.

I maintain my previous stance that YMAX offers a layer of diversification to the risks present in these kinds of black-box strategies, because it spreads out the risk across several instruments, which reduces the overall effect of the managers being right or wrong about a particular stock.

However, one has to trust in YieldMax to some degree, as even holding its funds in aggregate can't diversify out the risk that their strategy isn't any good. While it has performed well so far, particularly in regard to YMAX, that is no guarantee of the future.

Inherited Fees

The fund itself has a high fee, mostly because it comes with its own management fee on top of the fees present in the underlying funds. The underlying funds contribute 0.99% to the fund's annual fee, and YMAX itself charges a 0.29% fee, bringing the total up to 1.28%. This may be considered too high by some investors, especially those who are willing to do the extra work of owning the individual funds and rebalancing themselves to save the 29bp. That's not me, as I'd rather pay the fee to have it managed neatly in a single ETF, but there are some, and it is worth mentioned because of that.

Inconsistent Yield

The last major risk for investors to consider is that the high income is very volatile. Because the underlying funds are all very volatile in themselves, they transfer that up to the fund-of-funds. YMAX's dividend cannot be more consistent than the aggregate of the underlying funds, because the fund doesn't hold back any cash that is paid out; it is all passed through to shareholders.

Note that the fund used to do monthly dividends instead of weekly, and so I am shortening this chart to just include the weekly distributions. They range anywhere from $0.10/share to $0.30/share, which is a massive gap.

Investors need to consider this carefully when investing in YMAX, as the fund may not maintain any given level of distributions, and the income generated by the fund all depends on the performance of the black-box options strategy YieldMax employs. This circles back to the first risk, active management risk.

Regarding the distributions themselves, we see that YMAX distributes primarily investment income, but does have a considerable amount of return of capital to distribute as well. This is an old 19a-1 form dating back to October, because I cannot find any newer forms on their website for any of their funds.

Suitability

YMAX has impressed me with its performance since my last rating, and I am maintaining my buy rating for it. One does need to consider the risks that this is a very volatile instrument and is only suitable for aggressive investors.

To this end, I am maintaining my recommendation for aggressive investors to avoid any single-stock YieldMax fund and instead allocate entirely to YMAX or the other fund-of-funds I've given a buy to, YMAG.

I advise that aggressive investors consider no more than a 5% allocation to YMAX, and to carefully consider the risk of underperformance and loss moving forward.

The income is very high, and the opportunity for a repeat of its past is considerable for investors who are willing to take the risk to potentially see that reward.

Conclusion

Ultimately, the YieldMax Universe Fund of Option Income ETFs (YMAX) has impressed me with its performance since my last rating, and I am maintaining my buy rating for it. The fund is attractive from a diversification standpoint, dialing back the catastrophic risks present in the single-stock funds. Bundling these ultra strategies, designed to maximize yield, has created an index fund of sorts that is also able to have a very high yield of nearly-48%.

Be careful with this fund, as a little can go a long way.

Thanks for reading.

data.title: Ariel Investments International Developed Markets Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Markets worldwide defied expectations in 2024 led by the dominating performance of U.S. mega-cap technology companies and investor enthusiasm for artificial intelligence (AI) themed stocks. However, persistent challenges in the Eurozone, including uncertainty around interest rates, concerns surrounding the new U.S. administration’s policies as well as a strengthening U.S. dollar drove the MSCI ACWI, MSCI ACWI ex-U.S. and MSCI EAFE Indices into the red. China’s economy also ended the year sluggishly, with September’s stimulus efforts losing steam and the recovery in consumer spending turning elusive. Although uncertainty and volatility are likely to remain elevated, the patient investor knows “stock prices trade on fundamentals. And when those solid fundamentals shine through, share prices rise. 1” Against this backdrop, the Ariel International (DM) Composite declined -4.98% gross of fees (-5.12% net of fees) in the quarter, ahead of both its primary and secondary benchmark, the MSCI EAFE and MSCI EAFE Value Indices, which returned -8.11% and -7.12%, respectively. Over the last one-year period, the Ariel International (DM) Composite advanced +7.56% gross of fees (+6.90% net of fees), outperforming both the MSCI EAFE Index and the MSCI EAFE Value Index, which returned +3.82% and +5.68%, respectively.

Ariel’s non-consensus approach seeks to identify undervalued, out-of-favor, franchises that are misunderstood and therefore mispriced. The Ariel International (DM) bottom-up strategy is overweight Consumer Discretionary, Utilities, Information Technology and Financials. The portfolio is meaningfully underweight Industrials, Energy, Consumer Staples and Communication Services and also lacks exposure to Materials. At the sector level, our stock selection within Health Care, Real Estate and Consumer Staples along with our avoidance of Materials were the largest sources of positive attribution. By comparison, our Consumer Discretionary holdings, allocation to currency forwards and positioning within Communication Services were our greatest detractors during the quarter.

Germany-based kidney dialysis services and products provider, Fresenius Medical Care AG (FMS)(OTCPK:FMCQF) advanced in the period following solid quarterly earnings results driven by an inflection in the U.S. market as treatment volumes returned to positive growth. Subsequently, management upgraded its 2024 outlook and reaffirmed guidance for 2025. Meanwhile, the company continues to successfully execute on its goal to exit non-core assets in Latin America and invest in Europe and Asia. Additionally, a new ruling from the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) for 2025 that increases payments to end-stage renal disease facilities further aided shares. At current valuation levels, we believe the Fresenius remains well-positioned from a risk/reward standpoint.

New holding, Italian bank, Banca Monte dei Paschi (OTCPK:BMDPF) also advanced following a quarterly earnings beat and subsequent raise in full year guidance. The Italian government also sold a portion of its stake in the firm further aiding share price appreciation. We believe the bank can provide long-term earnings stability through its recent actions to reduce interest rate sensitivity and improve asset quality, thereby enhancing profitability and the balance sheet. Additionally, the bank's increased payout ratio to 75% and attractive cash yield—the highest among European Union banks—bolsters our positive outlook. Furthermore, we think a favorable interest rate outlook alongside the company’s solid capital profile and robust trading book as well as the potential for consolidation in the Italian banking sector presents additional upside potential.

Additionally, Emaar Properties PJSC, a property investment and development company in the MENA region, traded higher over the period on strong earnings results, highlighted by record quarterly residential sales, healthy margins and robust free cash flow. Additionally, the company announced an attractive long-term dividend policy to return capital to shareholders. Looking ahead, we believe Emaar Properties will continue to benefit from the post COVID recovery in Dubai’s real estate market, which has been amplified by a large number of expatriates entering the country.

In contrast, French multinational tire manufacturer, Michelin (CDGE) was a notable underperformer in the period due to a challenging macro environment. Weaker-than-expected consumer demand caused a sharp decline in volumes resulting in to a top- and bottom-line earnings miss. Subsequently, management cut 2024 guidance and announced plans to close two of its factories by 2026 to reduce costs. Nonetheless, we remain enthusiastic about Michelin's strong global competitive position and pricing strategy. In our view, shares are trading at a large discount to historical multiples despite a stronger balance sheet, higher free cash flow generation and attractive returns of capital to shareholders.

French pharmaceutical company, Sanofi (SNY)(OTCPK:SNYNF) also underperformed. The new administration’s decision to appoint a well-known vaccine critic as the future Secretary of the U.S. Department of Health and Human Services (HHS) weighed on pharmaceutical shares industrywide. In our view, the sell-off is an overreaction, as the vaccine business is a small part of Sanofi’s total revenue. Moreover, vaccine effectiveness is supported by strong scientific evidence and real-world data, particularly for young, elderly and at-risk patients. At current valuation levels, we view Sanofi's vaccine business and its Respiratory Syncytial Virus (RSV) Antibody as underappreciated and believe the immunology pipeline continues to be overlooked. Also, we remain optimistic Dupixent will be a key long-term growth driver.

Finally, China-based E-commerce company, JD.com, Inc. (JD)(OTCPK:JDCMF) detracted from performance in the period. The stock came under pressure as some investors took profits on solid earnings performance, while others became concerned with the implications tariffs could have on the Chinese economy. In our view, this share price action runs counter to the company's solid business fundamentals. The home appliance trade-in program and popular shopping event, Singles' Day, generated significant consumer spending across various product categories. Additionally, the company’s strategic decision to diversify general merchandise product offerings, expand its third-party marketplace business and monetize advertising streams continues to aid the top- and bottom-lines. Despite the near-term noise, we continue to view the company’s strategic positioning favorably and like JD.com’s long-term growth prospects.

In addition to purchasing Italian bank, Banca Monte dei Paschi, we initiated seven new positions in the quarter.

We added global bank and financial services provider, Barclays PLC (BCS)(OTCPK:BCLYF). We expect shares to benefit from a recovery in global capital markets and net interest income (NII) growth driven by macroeconomic hedging and asset flows. The bank is also planning to expand its investment banking advisory business. Moreover, its U.S. credit card business presents opportunities for either a potential sale or a quicker earnings recovery. Taken together, we see a reasonable path for Barclays to pursue its return targets, which include the distribution of £3 billion and £10 billion to shareholders through dividends and share repurchases between 2024 and 2026 and achieving a return on tangible common equity of about 12%.

We initiated a position in specialty insurance company, Beazley plc (OTCPK:BZLYF)(OTCPK:BZLEY). The firm’s diversified portfolio offerings include commercial property, specialty risk and cyber insurance. We find the growth and profitability profile of its property segment to be attractive as 100% of its business in North America is being generated by the booming Excess and Surplus (E&S) market—an area that caters to non-traditional, high-risk insurance needs. Cyber also presents an exciting structural growth opportunity for the company, particularly since insurance penetration of information assets is low.

Looking ahead, we find Beazley’s earnings outlook to be attractive, supported by its robust business mix, solid margins and improving capital profile.

We also bought leading European telecommunications services operator, Orange SA (OTC:ORANY). The company offers mobile, fixed-line and broadband services to roughly 300 million customers across 26 countries. Orange SA has a strong market presence in France and Spain and is expanding operations across the Middle East and Africa (MEA). We believe positive subscriber trends and rational price increases across these markets will support long term revenue growth. For instance, Orange is gaining market share on its network and service quality in France; benefitting from merger synergies and monetizing the fiber business in Spain; and offering mobile data services to significantly under-penetrated regions in MEA. Meanwhile, as the company completes its fiber-roll-out in France, we expect higher profitability, free cash flow generation and increasing shareholder returns.

We purchased Redeia Corp SA (OTCPK:RDEIF)(OTCPK:RDEIY), a Spain-based electricity provider. We believe Redeia’s ability to generate robust earnings growth with further upside from additional regulatory drivers is currently being underappreciated by the market.

We added shares of leading pure-play producer of memory semiconductor products, SK Hynix Inc. (OTCPK:HXSCF) In our view, SK Hynix is well positioned to benefit from increased deployment of its leading-edge High Bandwidth Memory (HBM) products, which are experiencing rapid demand growth to power Artificial Intelligence (AI).

We initiated a position in Sega Sammy Holdings Inc. (OTCPK:SGAMY)(OTCPK:SGAMF), a Japanese holding company specializing in the development of video games and the production of pachinko and pachislot machines. We believe the company is poised to deliver higher earnings, driven by robust growth within its video game segment. This is largely due to the company’s new multiplatform storytelling strategy centered around the firm’s core intellectual property: Sonic, Atlus, and Like a Dragon.

We believe it will help drive sales of both old and new titles, thereby boosting margins. Meanwhile, the pachinko and pachislot segment remains a profitable and stable cash flow generator for the company.

And finally, we purchased Vanguard FTSE Developed Markets ETF (VEA) as a cash alternative during the quarter.

By comparison, we successfully exited German reinsurer, Munich Re. and high-tech solutions and services provider in the defense and satellite space, Thales SA (OTCPK:THLEF)(OTCPK:THLLY) on valuation. We also sold British home and auto insurer, Direct Line Insurance Group Plc (OTCPK:DIISF)(OTCPK:DIISY) and Korean-based leading manufacturer of mobile phones and smartphones, Samsung Electronics Company, Ltd (OTCPK:SSNLF) to pursue more compelling opportunities.

The stock market dynamics of 2024 highlight a transformative shift in the global economy, fueled by advancements in artificial intelligence and geopolitical fragmentation. While the narrow ascent of AI themed stocks have driven market returns in recent years, opportunities should broaden to companies utilizing AI to create real-world efficiencies. As we head into 2025, there may be an over-reliance on the U.S. stock market to drive returns. With uncertainty surrounding rate cuts as well as the policy agenda for the new US administration, we expect heightened market volatility. We see this environment as conducive for active managers, whose knowledge, expertise and forward thinking enables them to look beyond the short-term noise and identify opportunities.

Accordingly, we are finding many mispriced global and international stocks trading at a discount to their U.S. counterparts. Their valuation is attractive, profitability less vulnerable and balance sheets remain strong. Against this backdrop, our international and global portfolios are well-positioned to deliver stronger returns over the long run.

Footnote

1 Hobson, Mellody and John W. Rogers Jr. “What the Stock market Taught Us This Year: Don’t Fall for These Investing Traps.” Wall Street Journal, 5 December 2023.

Investments in non-U.S. securities may underperform and may be more volatile than comparable U.S. stocks because of the risks involving non-U.S. economies, markets, political systems, regulatory standards, currencies and taxes. The use of currency derivatives and ETFs may increase investment losses and expenses and create more volatility. Investments in emerging markets present additional risks, such as difficulties in selling on a timely basis and at an acceptable price. The intrinsic value of the stocks within the strategy may never be recognized by the broader market. The strategy is often concentrated in fewer sectors than its benchmarks, and its performance may suffer if these sectors underperform the overall stock market.

Past performance does not guarantee future results. For the period ended 12/31/2024, the performance (net of fees) for the Ariel International (DM) Composite for the 1, 5, and 10- year periods were +6.90%, +3.42%, and +4.10%, respectively. For the period ended 12/31/2024 the performance for the MSCI EAFE Index and the MSCI EAFE Value Index for the 1, 5, and 10-year periods were +3.82%, +4.73%, and +5.20% and +5.68%, +5.09%, and +4.31%, respectively. Ariel Composite Net of Fees returns are calculated by deducting: (1) for the period from inception to December 31, 2013, the maximum advisory fee in effect for the respective period, applied on a monthly basis; and (2) for the period from January 1, 2014 onwards, the actual monthly advisory fee (on an asset-weighted basis) accrued for the accounts in the composite, using the fee rates in place as of the most recent calendar quarter-end. Gross returns do not reflect the deduction of advisory fees. Client returns will be reduced by advisory fees and such other expenses as may be incurred in the management of the account. Advisory fees are described in Part 2 of Ariel’s Form ADV. Returns assume the reinvestment of dividends and other earnings. Returns are expressed in U.S. dollars. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data quoted. The Ariel International (DM) Composite differs from its benchmark, the MSCI EAFE Index, because: (i) the Composite has fewer holdings than the benchmark and (ii) the Composite will at times invest a portion of its assets in the U.S. and emerging markets.

The opinions expressed are current as of the date of this commentary but are subject to change. The information provided in this commentary does not provide information reasonably sufficient upon which to base an investment decision and should not be considered a recommendation to purchase or sell any particular security. There is no guarantee that any of the views expressed will come to fruition or any investment will perform as described.

As of 12/31/2024, Fresenius Medical Care AG constituted 4.5% of the Ariel International (DM) Composite (representative portfolio); Banca Monte dei Paschi di Siena SpA 2.8%; Emaar Properties (P.J.S.C) 1.6%; Michelin 4.2%; Sanofi 5.0%; JD.com, Inc. 2.1%; Barclays PLC 2.7%; Beazley PLC 1.6%; Orange SA 1.6%; Redeia Corp SA 2.7%; SK Hynix Inc. 1.0%; Sega Sammy Holdings Inc. 2.0%; Vanguard FTSE Developed Markets ETF 0.1%; Direct Line Insurance Group PLC 0.0%; Munich Re 0.0%; Samsung Electronics Co Ltd. 0.0%; and Thales SA 0.0%. The performance of any single portfolio holding is no indication of the performance of other portfolio holdings of the Ariel International (DM) Composite.

A glossary of financial terms provided herein may be found on our website at www.arielinvestments.com.

Indexes are unmanaged. An investor cannot invest directly in an index. The MSCI EAFE® Index is an equity index of large and mid-cap representation across 21 Developed Markets (DM) countries around the world, excluding the U.S. and Canada. Its inception date is May 31, 1986. The MSCI EAFE Value Index captures large and mid-cap securities exhibiting overall value style characteristics across Developed Markets countries around the world, excluding the US and Canada. Its inception date is December 8, 1997. All MSCI Index net returns reflect the reinvestment of income and other earnings, including the dividends net of the maximum withholding tax applicable to non-resident institutional investors that do not benefit from double taxation treaties. MSCI uses the maximum tax rate applicable to institutional investors, as determined by the company’s country of incorporation. MSCI makes no express or implied warranties or representations and shall have no liability whatsoever with respect to any MSCI data contained herein. The MSCI data may not be further redistributed or used to create indices or financial products.

This report is not approved or produced by MSCI.

Investments in non-U.S. securities may underperform and may be more volatile than comparable U.S. stocks because of the risks involving non-U.S. economies, markets, political systems, regulatory standards, currencies and taxes. The use of currency derivatives and ETFs may increase investment losses and expenses and create more volatility. Investments in emerging markets present additional risks, such as difficulties in selling on a timely basis and at an acceptable price. The intrinsic value of the stocks within the strategy may never be recognized by the broader market. The strategy is often concentrated in fewer sectors than its benchmarks, and its performance may suffer if these sectors underperform the overall stock market.

Past performance does not guarantee future results. Performance results are shown net of the highest management fee charged to any client in the Composite during the performance period. Net returns reflect performance returns after the deduction of advisory fees and transaction costs and assume the reinvestment of dividends and other earnings. For the period ended 6/30/2024, the performance (net of fees) for the Ariel International (DM) Composite for the 1, 5, and 10-year periods were +6.34%, +3.76%, and +2.62%, respectively. For the period ended 6/30/2024 the performance for the MSCI EAFE Index and the MSCI EAFE Value Index for the 1, 5, and 10-year periods were +11.54%, +6.46%, and +4.33% and +13.75%, +6.06%, and +3.01%, respectively. Actual fees may vary depending on, among other things, the applicable fee schedule and portfolio size. Fee information is available upon request and may also be found in Ariel Investments LLC's Form ADV, Part 2. Returns are expressed in U.S. dollars.

Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data quoted. The Ariel International (DM) Composite differs from its benchmark, the MSCI EAFE Index because: (i) the Composite has fewer holdings than the benchmark and (ii) the Composite will at times invest a portion of its assets in the U.S. and emerging markets.

The opinions expressed are current as of the date of this commentary but are subject to change. The information provided in this commentary does not provide information reasonably sufficient upon which to base an investment decision and should not be considered a recommendation to purchase or sell any particular security. There is no guarantee that any of the views expressed will come to fruition or any investment will perform as described.

As of 6/30/2024, Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co., Ltd. ADR constituted 1.8% of the Ariel International (DM) Composite (representative portfolio); Tecnicas Reunidas SA 0.8%; Michelin (CDGE) 6.3%; Stellantis NV 3.0%; Daimler Truck Holding AG 2.9%; Baidu, Inc. ADR 1.8%; AXA SA 1.2%; BAWAG Group AG. 2.3%; Emaar Properties PJSC 0.5%; Infineon Technologies AG 2.5%; Grupo Financiero Banorte SAB de CV 0.0%; and Vodafone Group PLC 0.0%. The performance of any single portfolio holding is no indication of the performance of other portfolio holdings of the Ariel International (DM) Composite.

A glossary of financial terms provided herein may be found on our website at www.arielinvestments.com.

Indexes are unmanaged. An investor cannot invest directly in an index.
