Its inception date is May 31, 1986. The MSCI EAFE Value Index captures large and mid-cap securities exhibiting overall value style characteristics across Developed Markets countries around the world, excluding the US and Canada. Its inception date is December 8, 1997. All MSCI Index net returns reflect the reinvestment of income and other earnings, including the dividends net of the maximum withholding tax applicable to non-resident institutional investors that do not benefit from double taxation treaties. MSCI uses the maximum tax rate applicable to institutional investors, as determined by the company’s country of incorporation. MSCI makes no express or implied warranties or representations and shall have no liability whatsoever with respect to any MSCI data contained herein. The MSCI data may not be further redistributed or used to create indices or financial products.

This report is not approved or produced by MSCI.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: ASML: Factoring In Every Possible Scenario - Potential Outweighs Downside
data.description:
There have been plenty of analysis here on SeekingAlpha covering ASML (NASDAQ:ASML; OTCPK:ASMLF) in February. They range from Deep Value Investing's Strong Sell over Best Anchor Stocks' Hold rating to Oliver Rodzianko's Strong Buy.

In this article, we are going to analyse all possible scenarios for ASML's future to factor in potential growth prospects, risks and the China factor.

Quick Overview

ASML is the dominant player in the lithography equipment market. They are currently holding a monopoly for the critical Extreme Violet Lithography (EUV) machines, used for the most sophisticated semiconductors. They are mainly delivering their machines to TSMC (TSM), Intel (INTC) and Samsung (OTCPK:SSNLF).

The exports of the EUV machines to China have already been restricted to slow China's development of sophisticated chips. The US however published new and updated regulations on exports further restricting technologies. This could in turn negatively impact ASML's existing exports to China, should the Dutch government once again comply to the US guidelines. According to management these new restrictions have no impact on the business and they reiterate the existing guidance for 2025 and 2030.

Considering that - as of Q4/24 - 27% of ASML's revenue was generated in China, investors and bearish SA analysts are understandably worried that potential trade restrictions could weigh heavily on the company's future.

To analyse the potential downside in this scenario, we will first take a look at the regular Bear, Base and Bull Cases.

Regular Scenarios

For these scenarios, we can mainly rely on the latest guidance for 2025 and 2030 of the management.

2025

For 2025, the company presented the following outlook in their latest earnings call:

This results in the following anticipations for our three cases:

2025 Revenue [EUR] Gross margin
Bear 30 billion 51%
Base 32.5 billion 52%
Bull 35 billion 53%

2030 and beyond

For 2030 management gave the following outlook on the investor day in 2024:

Taking these assumptions, we get the following revenue growth rates and margins for 2030.

Revenue 2030 [EUR] Resulting CAGR Gross Margin 2030
Bear 44 billion ~8% 56%
Base 52 billion ~10% 58%
Bull 60 billion ~11% 60%

I also assumed that the predicted CAGR will stay at these rates for 2031 and 2032 to expand our analysis time horizon to eight years.

General Anticipations

To eventually evaluate the company in the three cases we need further assumptions. These stayed constant across all scenarios:

  • Gross Profit to EBIT conversion: Here I averaged out the last three years and used 62% for every year of our analysis.
  • EBIT to Free Cash Flow conversion: I once again averaged out the last three years and therefore calculated 88% as the EBIT to FCF conversion rate. This is also in line with other past metrics. (2018: 87%, 2019: 90%, 2020: 85%)
  • WACC: I assumed 8.0% as ASML's WACC as this is right around the current rate.
  • Perpetuity Growth Rate: 3.5% seems reasonable here.

Bear-Case

With these assumptions and the low range of management's expectations, we get a fair value market cap of $280 billion, indicating that the company could be overvalued by just 5% in the regular Bear case.

Base-Case

Assuming the middle of management's expectations for 2025 and 2030, we get a fair value market cap of $355 billion, indicating a potential upside of 21%.

Bull-Case

Calculating with the high end of the outlook, we get a fair value market cap pf $420 billion, resulting in a potential undervaluation of 42%.

The China Factor

Like mentioned in the beginning there is a realistic risk, that the US could try to prohibit ASML from supplying the less sophisticated DUV machines to China. ASML is currently generating a meaningful share of their revenues through China, especially in 2023 (29%) and 2024 (41%) this proportion was significant.

ASML's CEO claims that this share should normalize in the following years to ~20%, because of backlogs in the previous years. To nevertheless factor in the worst case, I calculated the Bear, Base and Bull Case with losing 27% of the revenue in 2025 due to trade restrictions (27% was the China share last quarter). The other assumptions were identical to our regular scenarios. This lead to the following valuations for ASML:

Bear w/o China: $206 billion - 30% overvaluation

Base w/o China: $262 billion - 11% overvaluation

Bull w/o China: $308 billion - 5% undervaluation

So even without any business in China, the company could still be trading in the somewhat fair value range.

How Realistic Is The Ban?

The Netherlands needs to protect its own economic interests while trying to balance their commitment to both Europe and the United States. By the implementation of export restrictions on Nvidia (NVDA) chips for example and requesting that ASML cease supplying its cutting-edge chip-making machinery to China, the United States seeks to restrict China's access to AI-related technologies. Nonetheless the Netherlands is wary about enforcing export restrictions that would needlessly hurt its own economy.

Trump's harsh economic and external policy could also lead to more cooperation between China and Europe.

I personally believe that the likelihood of an extension of the ban is very low. The provision of the DUV machines in no way represents such a competitive advantage as the EUV machines from ASML, which makes the ban pointless.

Fell free to comment your thoughts on this situation!

Conclusion

To summarize we have the following under- and overvaluations for ASML considering our six scenarios:

Bear Case Base Case Bull Case
Regular -5% +21% +42%
w/o China -30% -11% +5%

Even in the rather unrealistic scenario, of ASML's future without any business in China, the company is reasonably valued taking the Bear and Bull case into account. Due to this and the high quality business model, with long term tailwinds in AI and the unique market position as a monopoly controlling one of the key technology (EUV) for the next decade, I currently rate the company a Strong Buy, with a medium-term price target of $850.

What’s Your Perspective? Let’s Discuss!

Thank you for reading my analysis! I'd love to hear your ideas and points of view — whether you agree, disagree, or have a unique take on the stock. Let's start a discussion in the comments! Also, please consider following and positively rating my article if you enjoyed the read! Thanks!

~ Fabian | FF

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Cameco: Crashing Uranium Spot Price Points To Stagnant Income
data.description:
Cameco (NYSE:CCJ) reported its Q4 earnings results on Friday with a non-GAAP EPS of C$0.36 and a revenue of C$1.18B, up about 40% from last year. However, its income was lower in 2024 than in 2023, mainly due to one-time charges related to the Westinghouse acquisition. The company's report had a positive tone, pointing to record production at McArthur/Key Lake and substantial contracting volumes; however, its output in Inkai Kazakhstan was lower due to ongoing supply chain issues. Inkai's production was suspended on January 1st due to documentation issues but was restarted at the end of the month, with its guidance still marred by sulfuric acid shortages.

Although its report was decent, investors commented negatively on its forward guidance. Its 2024 contract volumes were lower than 2023, offsetting higher contract prices while concerns mounted regarding expected upcoming ~10% tariffs on Canadian uranium exports (10% on energy and 25% on other). Ongoing issues and a general lack of clear messaging and communication surrounding production issues in Inkai appear to be the most significant concern, as production shortfalls and outages could create problems in fulfilling its contracts. As a result, CCJ crashed about 6.6% on Friday, leaving it down over 15% since 2025 began.

This negative shift is not a surprise to me. In November, I published "Cameco: Strong Q3 Performance As Kazakh Output Falls Short." CCJ has fallen around 19% since then and is now trading around the price it was when I became bearish last March. In the latest article, I warned investors that the company faced long-lasting supply-chain issues in Kazakhstan JV (Inkai) due to the country's sulfuric acid shortage. However, Kazakhstan's production shortfalls may support higher uranium prices, improving Cameco's performance from Canadian operations.

With tariffs now a key factor, Cameco may face some issues in contracting with the US because tariff costs are pushed onto customers, making the company's exports less competitive. With this in mind, the outlook for Cameco and the uranium market is shifting, potentially leaving CCJ overvalued.

The Uranium Shortage is Over, Despite Inkai

Around a year ago, many investors were extremely bullish on the nuclear and uranium industry due to a sharp increase in uranium prices in late 2023. I was among a minority of (previously bullish) uranium analysts sounding a bearish signal despite uranium trading over $100/lbs. I believe North American uranium mine production would rise if prices were over ~$90/lbs, given that is roughly the breakeven cost in North America. Given Cameco's reported average unit cost was around $46, and much of its production was from Kazakhstan (in 2022), the breakeven cost in Kazakhstan is likely below that figure.

As a result of higher prices, 2024 saw a surge in mine development and exploration activity in the US. The sharp rise in prices was driven by previous shutdowns in Cameco's higher-cost Canadian mines during the previously unprofitable period, marked by COVID lockdowns and related issues that made it challenging for Cameco to fulfill contract obligations, leading Cameco to purchase uranium to resell to fill contracts. However, that shortage may return to a glut now that US miners are producing more and its Canadian mines are producing record amounts. The spot market price of uranium has collapsed to ~$65/lb. See below:

In my view, the uranium market is one of the most supply-driven. Many analysts and investors point to efforts to increase US nuclear energy production as a bullish case for uranium. However, relatively bullish estimates place global uranium consumption growth at around 28% from 2023 to 2033, or around 2.5% annually. The outlook beyond that is unclear due to the need to retire many older nuclear plants in North America and Europe.

There are very few nuclear projects in the US pipeline, and previously completed projects were vastly over budget and delayed. Trump's pro-nuclear energy secretary may push for more projects, but the timeline from the concept of a project to completion is likely at least two decades.

Further, while fusion energy has been a "hot air" subject for decades, France's current project recently completed a 22-minute fusion reactor run, beating China's January results by 25%. While speculative, we may be reaching an exponential growth rate in this emerging technology. It can produce magnitudes more power than nuclear power, potentially making it a more viable long-term investment if we look out two to three decades in the future.

In short, it is not true that uranium consumption will rise significantly this decade, and probably unlikely it will increase considerably in the 2030s-2040s. At the same time, uranium is relatively abundant, and Cameco's efforts to improve its production show that supply can quickly ramp up in the event of higher prices. I believe the only way to sustain prices over $90 would be for continued supply chain and export issues in Kazakhstan, where uranium can be mined very cheaply. The country has had shortfalls due to a local lack of sulfuric acid over the past two years, contributing to the shortage. Still, with tariffs coming on Canada, Kazakh mines will have a slight pricing edge in 2025. In 2026, Kazatomprom aims to complete a sulfuric acid plant in Kazakhstan, which should stabilize the country's production. That benefits Cameco's JV Inkai but may also push supply above demand.

Cameco Profit Outlook 2025-2030

Although CCJ is highly correlated to the spot price of uranium, its EPS reacts relatively slowly to uranium prices because most of its output is under long-term contracts. In 2024, the company secured decent contracting volumes at much higher prices, however, it's still relatively exposed to today's lower prices. The uranium spot is currently at $65, indicating Cameco's realized price should be around $55-$65 over the coming years. See its updated sensitivity table:

With uranium's spot price at its current level, Cameco's contract price curve is relatively flat. However, if prices fall or rise significantly, the curve is expected to steepen, limiting its exposure to a significant move in uranium prices.

Cameco's average 2024 unit cost of sales, including depreciation, was ~C$59.5, up 11% from C$53.4 in 2023. That translates to about $42/lb in USD, up from ~$37 the year prior. With spot prices where they are, we should expect realized prices around $60-$65/lb over the coming years, giving it a margin of about $20-$25/lb. That said, its unit costs did rise 11% last year, which is faster than inflation, so it is likely that Cameco's margin will decline over the coming years unless spot prices rebound.

Overall, this translates to an expected operating income on its uranium segment of around $440M to $625M, depending on its ability to grow volume or if that volume will decline due to Inkai's issues. Its adjusted EBITA on fuel services has been around $50M and is relatively steady, though Westinghouse is $162M, up 60% YoY as demand for non-Russian enrichment grows. Adding those up, I estimate CCJ's EBITDA should be around $800M to $1B over the coming years. That translates to an expected income of around $475M after accounting for interest, depreciation, and taxes or an EPS of about $1.1.

The current consensus EPS estimate for CCJ in 2025 is $0.95, rising to $2.96 by the decade's end. My estimate is similar, but I do not expect a significant increase in its EPS above ~$1.5 given the recent sharp declines in uranium spot prices, which I expect will be maintained given potential glut dynamics. Westinghouse is a key growth opportunity, but I do not think it is sufficient to achieve such strong income growth. Overall, I think the analyst consensus fails to account for the recent sharp decline in uranium spot price and the likelihood that Cameco's unit costs may rise over the coming years while spot prices may stagnate.

The Bottom Line

I believe that CCJ is overvalued, given its forward "P/E" ratio is likely around 40X. In my view, that would only be reasonable if we were confident that its EPS would rise quickly over the coming years. That is what many other analysts expect, so I do not think it is worth short-selling CCJ today. Still, I see more downside risk in uranium than upside opportunity, barring potential geopolitical or internal events that create supply chain issues in Kazakhstan. If Kazakh uranium supplies become more limited for whatever reason (Russia), uranium could have immense upside. That hedging opportunity may justify CCJ's high valuation, but I wouldn't bet on that outcome for now.

Further, I do not see a tremendous long-term opportunity in Cameco at its current price. The long-term supply and demand outlook for uranium appears relatively neutral, with supply quickly growing to fulfill moderate demand growth expectations. In the long term, I think Cameco's Canadian mines risk becoming unprofitable if their costs rise disproportionately to the overall inflation rate. Demand for uranium may increase with nuclear investment, but likely not until the mid to late 2030s, and even then, there seems to be far more talk and hype around nuclear than actual project planning.

data.title: MongoDB: Wait For Results While Strategic Shift In Transition
data.description:
Introduction and Investment Thesis

MongoDB (NASDAQ:MDB) is one of the most popular NoSQL databases. MDB had gained popularity by appealing to application developers, by allowing access to its free version of Community Server, eventually converting them to paid customers. Their revenue growth % had slowed down from high 40s in FY22 and FY23 to high-teens in the three quarters of FY25. However, MongoDB is making the right pivot to focus more on Enterprise customers by investing in sales and channel resources to bring back the past momentum. The industry trend of increased AI spend in the next few years is also a potential growth opportunity for MongoDB. However, the real test is ahead as MDB must compete with incumbent Enterprise behemoths and Cloud providers with their own data storage platforms. Heading into Q4 FY25 earnings, a Hold rating is appropriate until we start seeing results from their Enterprise expansion strategy.

MongoDB Product-Market Fit (Relational vs NoSQL Databases)

For a large portion of computing history, databases are designed as a set of inter-related (hence 'Relational') tables with data stored in rows and columns. Some of the most popular Relational databases include Oracle, MySQL (also owned by Oracle), Microsoft SQL, and PostgreSQL. All the relational databases are based on a specific query language called SQL. With the advent of big data, cloud computing, and other modern application needs, a new design pattern for databases emerged in the form of NoSQL ('non-relational' or 'not only SQL'). Unlike Relational databases, NoSQL databases do not have a fixed schema (a rigid structure to store data) - they can cater to large volumes of unstructured and semi-structured data and can easily scale to growing data needs.

There are four primary data models within NoSQL databases:

  1. Document stores: Data is stored as a collection of documents which are typically in JSON format (series of key/value pairs). Major players include MongoDB, Amazon DynamoDB/DocumentDB, Apache CouchDB
  2. Key-value stores: Data is stored as simple key-value pairs. Mainly used for caching, fast key-based queries. Major players include Amazon DynamoDB, Redis.
  3. Wide column/Columnar stores: Data is stored in columns as opposed to rows in a traditional database. Primarily used for data analytics and warehousing. Major players include Apache Cassandra, Apache HBase.
  4. Graph databases: Data is stored in a graph structure as nodes and edges instead of tables or documents. Major players include Neo4j, Amazon Neptune, ArangoDB.

Based on the application needs, the choice of data model could vary, and within a given data model, multiple vendor choices are available in market. In the recent past, many vendors have been offering multi-model data stores (e.g., document & key-value, or graph & key-value) to serve emerging data needs for their customers. Microsoft's Azure Cosmos DB, Amazon's DynamoDB, MongoDB (document + vector), Couchbase are a few examples.

Despite the plethora of NoSQL options, Relational databases still dominate the market (as shown below). The major reasons include continued relevance of SQL in data analytics, SQL's compatibility with emerging data ecosystems, and application developer's comfort and expertise in using Relational databases.

MDB's Financials and Growth Opportunities

MongoDB has two primary product offerings - Atlas, a DBaaS offer available in the 3 major clouds (AWS, Azure, GCP) hosted by MongoDB for its customers; Enterprise Advanced, a self-serve offer that customers typically deploy on their premises and manage it themselves. Most of MDB's recent growth came from Atlas (representing 72% and 68% in Q1-Q3 FY25 and FY24 respectively vs 58% in FY22).

On the customers front, MongoDB had a total of 52,600 paying customers as of Q3 FY25. However, we should pay attention to their Direct Sales (DS) customers who typically are larger enterprise customers holding relationship with MongoDB's sales force. These DS customers contributed 88% of subscription revenue despite being 15% (7,000) of total customer base. As of Q3 FY25, they had 7,400 DS customers representing 88% of subscription revenue for the three quarters of FY25.

As can be seen from above, MongoDB's revenue growth had been decelerating in the last few years, despite healthy growth in Atlas (DBaaS) revenue. Note that part of the Atlas growth is from the Enterprise Advanced (on-prem) customers expanding workloads onto Atlas (a bit of cannibalization). To bring back the growth from FY23 and prior, MongoDB must win increased share of workloads from enterprise customers. I believe their plan to invest further in the sales force to expand the enterprise footprint is the right strategic move, as their penetration with Fortune 100 and 500 companies is a mere 2% as of May 2024.

As with most firms in the Tech industry now, AI is a potential tailwind for MongoDB. They claim a few customer case studies (like Novo Nordisk) who built Gen AI applications leveraging MongoDB. However, I think that the tech stack (collection of tools) for AI applications is still evolving as enterprises experiment with applying Gen AI to their needs, and the wide variety of data platform options (as listed in the Background section) also makes it difficult for customers to narrow down to MongoDB.

Risks for MongoDB's Growth

Following are the three major risks I see for MongoDB's future growth:

1. Cloud Providers: I consider the competition from hyperscalers to be the biggest risk for MongoDB. Data platforms like AWS DynamoDB and Azure Cosmos DB have an inherent advantage of being the first choice for customers using their Cloud infrastructure for rest of the application's needs. MongoDB's counter-argument that they could serve multiple clouds, in my view, is a weak one, as data needs tend to be localized at the application or application group level which typically are deployed together in one environment.

2. Mindshare from Enterprise Customers: Gaining mindshare (subsequent wallet-share) from enterprise customers is going to be an uphill battle due to entrenched vendors like Oracle and Microsoft with stronger relationship with enterprises through their data platform or other adjacent offerings.

3. Continued R&D and Sales expense impacting profitability: To gain access to enterprise customers, MongoDB's Sales & Marketing expense as a % of Sales will likely increase from their current 48%. Their R&D expense will likely (rather should) stay at 33% to keep up with ever changing competitive landscape. Both these factors will continue to put pressure on MDB's profitability, keeping them unprofitable in the foreseeable future.

MDB Stock Valuation

($ in '000) MongoDB Snowflake (SNOW) Atlassian (TEAM)
Cash $673,054 $4,156,990 $2,469,233
Debt $1,124,720 $2,595,628 $1,244,526
Market Cap $21,475,595 $61,769,427 $78,222,083
Enterprise Value $21,927,261 $60,208,065 $76,997,376
Sales $1,977,000 $3,430,000 $4,400,000
EV to Sales 11.09 17.55 17.50
Sales Growth 20% 29% 19%
EV to Sales to Growth 55.5 60.5 92.1

Based on the high-end of MDB's FY25 revenue guidance, their EV to Sales (MDB is not yet profitable) is 11.09. Also considering their growth rate of 20%, I believe MDB to be fairly or slightly undervalued compared to other SaaS vendors. But their decelerating growth is a cause for concern, and it is prudent to monitor whether they can bring back growth in the next few quarters.

Concluding Remarks on MongoDB

MongoDB is in the early phase of their strategic shift to focus on enterprise customers and making appropriate investments to meet that objective. Multiple puzzle pieces must come together for their success, including customer migration from Relational to NoSQL data stores, differentiating against Cloud Providers' native data platforms and other NoSQL platforms, to name a few. I recommend waiting to see positive results from execution before making or expanding on your investment in MDB.

data.title: BST: 7.5%+ Yield, 5% Discount On Tech
data.description:
Tech stocks may have captured much of the market's interest over the past decade, but they don't offer attractive dividends.

Many income investors turn to closed end funds, "CEF's", which handle the tech stock analysis, and market transactions. One of these funds, BlackRock Science and Technology Trust (NYSE:BST), offers investors an attractive yield, ~7.5%, on Tech and Science assets. It does this via selling covered call options on a portion of its portfolio.

When we last wrote about BST, in early October 2024, it was selling at a ~6% discount, and yielding 8.5%. Since then, it gained ~8%, and delivered 5 monthly distributions, for a total return of ~12%, in a bit over 4.5 months:

Fund Profile:

BST's investment objectives are "providing income and total return through a combination of current income, current gains and long-term capital appreciation. Under normal market conditions, the Trust will invest at least 80% of its total assets in equity securities issued by U.S. and non-U.S. science and technology companies in any market capitalization range, selected for their rapid and sustainable growth potential." (BST site)

BST's assets have grown by ~$100M during this period, to $1.4B, while its holdings have narrowed, to 91, vs. 96 in October. Average daily volume is also a bit lower, at 129K, vs. 144K in October. The expense ratio is stable, at 1.09%, while Covered Calls/Assets is also lower, at 26.50%, vs. 28% in October '24.

Dividends:

BST pays a monthly $.25 distribution. At its 2/21/25 closing price of $38.03, BST yielded 7.89%. BST generally goes ex-dividend in mid-month, and pays in the last week of the month.

BST has a strong 5-year dividend growth rate of nearly 19%, due to a major 107% rise in 2021. Management raised the monthly payout from $.226 to $.25 in Q4 2021, where it has remained.

Taxes:

BST's 2024 distributions were estimated to have come from 99% long-term gains, and 1% Return of Capital:

Holdings:

Tech formed ~75% of BST's portfolio, stable vs. 8/30/24, with Software & Services down ~120 basis points, Semi's down 103 basis points, and Tech Hardware up 179 basis points. Media & Entertainment rose 164 basis points.

As of 1/31/25, BST's top 10 holdings formed ~50% of its portfolio. Like so many other CEFs, there were many members of the so-called "Magnificent Seven" among BST's top 10.

NVIDIA was its biggest holding, at 11.85%, followed by Microsoft, at 8.24%, Apple, 7.62%.

Risks:

Tech Concentration: With Tech forming 75% of BST's portfolio, a down year for Tech, such as 2022, will pressure BST's shares, as you'll see in the following Performance section.

Rising Interest Rates: As noted previously, rising interest rates are seen as a negative factor for Tech stocks. That type of environment has pushed down BST's price/share in a big way in the past.

Owning 6 out of the 7 Magnificent 7 stocks was a good move in 2024, with all but MSFT posting outsized gains over the past year, in addition to Broadcom, which is up 78%.

So far in 2025, though, it's a different story—only META has beaten the market, rising 16.7%, whereas NVDA is flat, and the rest of this group is in the red, especially TSLA, which is down 16%:

Performance:

While BST lagged the broad Tech sector and the S&P 500 over the past year, it has outperformed them over the last quarter, and so far, in 2025:

BST had a 24% NAV return in 2024, with an 18% price return, indicating a discount to NAV. Its 3-year returns were negative, due to the decline in 2022, while its 5-year NAV return of 12.6% exceeded its price return of 10.24%.

BST's NAV and price returns were the closest in its 10-year returns, with NAV at 15.77%, and price at 15.99%:

BST outperformed the Morningstar Equity CEF category in 2017-2020, during a low interest rate period, but tanked in 2022, during a rapid rise in rates. It outperformed in 2023, and lagged in 2024.

Valuations:

Since NAV/Share is calculated at the end of each trading day, you have to look at the most recent closing values to determine the current NAV discount or premium. Buying CEF's at a deeper discount than their historical average discounts/premiums can be a useful strategy due to mean reversion.

At its 2/20/25 closing price of $38.72, BST was trading at a 5% discount to NAV/share, a bit deeper than its 1-year average of 4.2%, but much deeper of a discount than its 3- and 5-year average prices to NAV of -1.65% and -0.86%.

Parting Thoughts:

At $38.03, BST was 4% below its 52-week high and is closer to being overbought than oversold on its slow stochastic chart. We rate it a 'Hold' for now—given the current market volatility, you may be able to buy it soon at an even deeper discount to NAV.

All tables furnished by Hidden Dividend Stocks Plus, unless otherwise noted.

data.title: Clearwater Analytics Holdings: Growth Outlook Remains Very Robust
data.description:
Summary

Following my coverage on Clearwater Analytics Holdings (NYSE:CWAN) in December, which I recommended a buy rating due to my expectation that growth can continue to accelerate back to mid-20%, which will support valuation to stay >13x forward revenue, this post is to provide an update on my thoughts on the business and stock. In 4Q24. CWAN continues to deliver solid performance. With growth momentum remaining solid and an enlarged TAM and margin expansion outlook, I remain convinced in CWAN’s growth potential.

Investment thesis

CWAN reported a solid 4Q24 quarter, with revenue coming in at $126.5 million, marking 27.7% y/y growth and a clear beat against consensus estimates of ~$120 million. This growth is a meaningful acceleration from 3Q24’s 22.4% y/y growth, signaling strong underlying demand. More importantly, annual recurring revenue ("ARR") climbed to $474.9 million, up 25.3% y/y, compared to 23% in 3Q24 and 17% in 4Q23. Net revenue retention ("NRR") also hit a new high of 116%. If we piece all of these together, it points to a very strong growth momentum, in fact, an improving growth momentum. If CWAN can continue to achieve > 115% NRR and sustain its current pace of customer growth (grew 8.4% y/y in 4Q24), achieving mid-20% growth is not a problem at all.

Notably, CWAN's total addressable market ("TAM") is now a lot larger than the last time I wrote about it. The pending $1.5 billion acquisition of Enfusion, which is expected to close in 2Q25, is a game-changer for CWAN, in my opinion. Historically, CWAN's focus has been on investment accounting and reporting, but with Enfusion, CWAN now has front-office capabilities, particularly in order and execution management, and this significantly broadens CWAN’s TAM. Per management estimates, Enfusion expands CWAN’s TAM by $1.9 billion, and importantly, it opens the door for CWAN to penetrate the hedge fund space. My view is that with Enfusion, CWAN can now offer a front-to-back investment management platform, which reduces the need for customers to integrate multiple software solutions (this gels with the ongoing vendor consolidation trend, by the way). With this end-to-end solution, it puts CWAN in a better position to win larger deals and increase wallet share among asset managers (cross-sell front/back-end solutions). Additionally, Enfusion also opens the door for CWAN to expand to international markets, particularly in Europe and Asia (Enfusion has more presence here vs. CWAN).

As such, to me, the growth outlook remains very positive. But revenue growth is not the only attractive aspect about CWAN; CWAN’s ability to expand margin remains solid. Gross margin in 4Q24 expanded to 78.8%, up 180 bps y/y, and management is now guiding for CWAN to reach >80% gross margins over time, and if true, this means that adj EBITDA margin has more headroom to expand. I think CWAN results are proof that margins still have room to expand. In 4Q24, incremental adj EBITDA margin was ~43% vs. adj EBITDA margin of 33%, which I believe reflects the results from CWAN's investment in AI-driven automation. Management has been talking about this for the past few quarters—investing in GenAI to improve operational efficiencies, particularly in data reconciliation and compliance monitoring—and this has clearly paid off.

Finally, CWAN's free cash flow ("FCF") generation ability remains solid. Although the reported FCF was -$30 million in 4Q24, this was due to a one-time $72.5 million tax agreement settlement. Excluding this, FCF would’ve been $42.5 million, bringing total FY24 FCF to ~$142 million. This positive FCF generation ability plus CWAN's clean balance sheet (just $46 million debt vs. cash and cash equivalents of $255 million) gives CWAN ample flexibility to reinvest heavily for growth—either via sales and marketing or acquisitions. A good thing is that management indicated that it does not expect to take on significant incremental debt related to the Enfusion acquisition, so there shouldn’t be any worry related to a higher debt level.

Valuation

CWAN’s growth is firing on all cylinders, and with a larger TAM, I remain confident that CWAN can accelerate its growth back to 25% in the coming years. In my model, I have CWAN generating ~$842 million in FY27, and if growth can accelerate as I expect, I expect CWAN to minimally trade at where it trades today—13x forward revenue. I should also note that CWAN's profit profile will increase from here, and that should justify a higher multiple as well. Some investors may compare CWAN to Vertex (VERX) which trades at 9x and say that CWAN trades at a premium despite similar growth outlook. I note that CWAN has a higher margin profile (VERX gross margin is only low-60% vs. CWAN approaching >80%).

Risk

While CWAN’s growth outlook remains strong, there are some risks to consider. Firstly, the integration of Enfusion may see hiccups, and that could impact near-term revenue growth. Secondly, CWAN is indeed trading at a premium, and despite my view that it is justified, it also means that the market has high expectations. If CWAN misses expectations, the stock price could take a big hit. Specifically, if CWAN’s growth dips below 20%, the market is very likely to react negatively.

Conclusion

I remain buy-rated for CWAN. CWAN’s 4Q24 performance shows that revenue growth momentum is still strong. The pending acquisition of Enfusion is a game changer, in my view, for CWAN growth strength. Aside from these, the ability to expand margins further is also very positive. Hence, my view is that CWAN can continue to trade minimally at the current valuation multiple.

data.title: Brookdale Senior Living: Sky-High Debt Remains The Meaningful Impediment To Sustained Gains
data.description:
Intro

Our last update on Brookdale Senior Living Inc. (NYSE:BKD) was in early November of last year when we pointed to how the stock's soon-to-be-announced third-quarter earnings report (Released on 11/6/2024) would most likely lead to a continuation of the bearish trend. Although Q3 revenue came in line for the quarter, Q3 GAAP earnings missed estimates at -$0.22 per share. Our supposition transpired as shares continued to decline from $6.22 at our time of commentary (11/2/2024) to bottoming out at approximately $4.50 a share on the 10th of February this year.

So why did we foresee a continuation of Brookdale's downtrend? Well, as discussed in November, shares had already topped out in July of 2024 (failing to punch above overhead resistance) which triggered a sustained pattern of lower lows in the Senior-Living provider. Furthermore, substantial selling volume accompanied the down-move, pointing to a continuation of the trend post the release of the company's Q3-2024 numbers.

Just remember, irrespective of the actual numbers released in quarterly earnings reports, the market has already divulged all known information (especially on long-term charts) up until that point. This means, barring a huge surprise, long-term trends should continue, all things remaining equal.

Near-Term Tailwinds

Nevertheless, since shares have bottomed (2/10/2025), we have seen a distinct change in the stock's volume profile with the release of Brookdale's Q4 earnings (2/18/2025) adding to the renewed enthusiasm in this play. Although negative GAAP earnings (-$0.37) once more missed estimates by a sizeable difference, investors were drawn to the content of the report which surmised almost 6% RevPAR growth, net profit (loss) growth, a constructive lease alteration with Ventas, the spanning out of long-term debt and the beefing up on the company's assets by taking ownership of 11 former leased communities. Given recent RevPAR trends and the fact that the rolling over of 2027 debt combined with the lease alteration will most likely lead to more cash flow for BKD, investors liked what saw concerning Brookdale's forward-looking expectations.

We, however, continue to take a cautious stance in this play. The attractiveness here from an investor's standpoint is that BKD traded well above $8 a share just a few short years ago through the pandemic. However, despite the improvements management has made with respect to

  1. Securing better rates at its facilities
  2. Retaining associates who provide the most value
  3. Improving operational excellence,

investors overlook that there remains a wall of technical resistance to get shares trading back above the $8 level in due time (47% increase over the present price). Furthermore, BKD does not pay a dividend (meaning investors must depend on capital gains in the stock alone) plus the company's cost of capital continues to remain elevated by the prolonged elevated interest rate environment.

Valuation Is Not Cheap Despite Carnage Since Mid-2021

By far the most attractive valuation multiple, Brookdale has in its arsenal is its price-to-sales ratio. With shares on the slide in recent quarters coupled with the fact that revenues are expected to reach $3.22 billion in fiscal 2025, BKD's forward sales multiple comes in at a very keen 0.34 (Sector median comes in at 3.68).

However, sales (as alluded to above) are not converting to positive GAAP earnings and free cash flow irrespective of how management dresses up the company's EBITDA trends. To this point, Brookdale's trailing cash-flow multiple of 6.52 is meaningless in a way when you consider that well over $300 million of capital spending was utilised over the past four quarters. Suffice it to say, although recent RevPAR growth and the likes of the spanning out of debt & new lease arrangements may improve GAAP earnings & free cash-flow trends over the near term, Brookdale remains unprofitable which means more external measures (added debt, etc) will be needed for the foreseeable future (adversely affecting the company's valuation in the process).

Debt Concerns Have Not Dissipated

Many times in financial markets, shares of overly leveraged companies can spike to the upside when fresh funding is secured. Funding which essentially buys a company time to get its house in order is one thing, but the question investors should pose is whether anything has changed fundamentally at the firm. Therefore, let's bring Brookdale's leverage down to brass tax by looking at its debt trends up to Q4.

Net debt came in at close to $3.7 billion at the end of Q4, resulting in a reported annual leverage ratio of 10.4. Approximately $170 million surplus debt was issued in fiscal 2024 as management chose to protect its liquidity profile (BKD's present cash position comes in at approximately $329 million). Irrespective of how upcoming debt payments have been spanned out in time, BKD's leverage dwarfs the market cap of the company ($1.086 billion)

Furthermore, if we were to take a best-case scenario standpoint from a funding standpoint, we see that it would take 58+ years to pay off the company's outstanding debt with Brookdale's annual operating profit ($63 million). Therefore, what investors should ponder here is how long more than management kicks the can down the road. How would rising interest rates affect how creditors view BKD as a viable enterprise going forward? Interest expense currently surpasses operating profit by a factor of 4, meaning we fail to see how this company will not need major restructuring in the years to come.

Conclusion

Although shares of Brookdale Senior Living have been enjoying the momentum of late, we are reiterating our 'Hold' rating in this play. High debt, negative earnings and the lack of free cash flow demonstrate to us that upside traction remains limited in the Senior living provider. We look forward to continued coverage.

data.title: Berkshire Hathaway: Warren Buffett Is Teaching Us A Lesson (Downgrade To Hold)
data.description:
After a tumultuous four weeks for the S&P 500 (SPX) (SPY), I was really looking forward to none other than Warren Buffett’s annual letter that typically affords us helpful and constructive (life and investing) insights, while setting the stage for Berkshire Hathaway’s annual meeting (NYSE:BRK.A) (NYSE:BRK.B) later in May. With Berkshire stock having outperformed the Magnificent Seven dominated S&P 500, I think it’s an opportune moment for us to take a step back and consider the wisdom encapsulated in Buffett's missive, as SPX has failed to break decisively above the 6,100 levels since early December.

Investors have already been given a preamble into Buffett’s current thinking, as we assess the recent significant trades for Berkshire in Q4. While Buffett’s lack of repurchasing Berkshire stock in Q4 arguably highlights his assessment of elevated market valuations, he also made clear that Berkshire will maintain its preference in the allocation of capital into equities instead of cash and their equivalents, which I believe validates Buffett’s belief in the underlying long-term strength of the American equities market, downplaying the bearish prognisticators who think that we could be at the “cusp” of a long term bear market. As a reminder, Buffett highlighted:

Berkshire shareholders can rest assured that we will forever deploy a substantial majority of their money in equities – mostly American equities although many of these will have international operations of significance. Berkshire will never prefer ownership of cash-equivalent assets over the ownership of good businesses, whether controlled or only partially owned. - (Warren Buffett annual letter 2024)

I emphasized in my previous Berkshire article that investors must be careful about being struck with “analysis paralysis” into Buffett’s decision to sell more stock. I added that the reallocation suggests an astute move to lower concentration risks, which in itself is a prudent capital allocation strategy, and not immediately indicative of a bearish signal. Therefore, I believe Buffett’s assurance in his annual letter about Berkshire’s capital deployment demonstrates his conviction in an equity-heavy strategy, even though the lack of “bargains” has likely driven his decision to not buy back Berkshire stock in Q4, which is again a prudent move. It’s critical for investors not to misconstrue Buffett’s signal as bearish, and there couldn’t have been a clearer message from the Berkshire Chairman and CEO into staying invested in high quality, and well managed companies.

Berkshire’s decision to shed significant holdings of Apple Inc. (AAPL) and Bank of America (BAC) has likely led to substantial capital gains taxes in 2024, although they are still within the conglomerate’s top ten holdings, underlining Buffett’s conviction about their long-term potential. Hence, I urge investors to ignore the fallacy of buying into certain media bias that may have flagged his reduction in these companies as suggestive of red flags in his assessment of the market.

Also, Buffett has helped build up a substantial cash pile of $334B as of Q4, even though as it reduced the portfolio exposure of its publicly listed portfolio, which is currently valued at just over $295B. Hence, I believe Berkshire has a massive stockpile of ammo ready to deploy if needed to capitalize on steep market selloffs, proffering investors' confidence in the conglomerate’s ability to drive substantial shareholder value when the opportunity arises.

As a result, I believe it should not only help keep investors on board, but also drive higher conviction in value investors knowing that Buffett is careful and judicious in capital allocation decisions. And his overwhelming confidence in equities suggests Berkshire expects to continue spurring growth through its public and non-public holdings, reinvesting its profits, without the need to pander to the needs of income investors (since Berkshire doesn’t pay a dividend).

In addition, I think investors shouldn’t forget that Berkshire has also astutely hedged its marketable equity exposure with controlled businesses that generate substantial operating earnings. Insurance underwriting remains a core business for Berkshire, not just for its contribution to operating profits, but also the massive $171B in float that offers Berkshire a “very cheap” source of funds to generate investment income that has surged more than 48% year-on-year. Therefore, even as the growth in operating earnings for its controlled (main and other) and non-controlled businesses could slow, the demonstrated scale and underwriting capabilities in Berkshire’s insurance business will remain a significant competitive advantage.

However, recent climate catastrophes as seen in the recent wildfires have also injected new “unknown” algorithms into insurance underwriting for Berkshire’s P&C division, and even Buffett acknowledged that the long-term tail risks could be massive, as he emphasized:

Climate change may have been announcing its arrival. However, no “monster” event occurred during 2024. Someday, any day, a truly staggering insurance loss will occur – and there is no guarantee that there will be only one per annum. - (Buffett annual letter)

The silver lining underscoring Berkshire’s ability to manage risks well is its inherent posture toward risk management (i.e. aversion, but not paralysis), and its corroborated capabilities to deploy capital well to generate substantial investment returns over time. Coupled with its scale (to manage risks and pricing appropriately) and float, Buffett recognizes that it isn’t something that any insurer can replicate easily, validating the sustainable moat that has been painstakingly developed and strengthened over time, as he articulated:

No private insurer has the willingness to take on the amount of risk that Berkshire can provide. At times, this advantage can be important. But we also need to shrink when prices are inadequate. We must never write inadequately-priced policies in order to stay in the game. - (Buffett annual letter)

Still, Berkshire stock is now valued at just over 24.4x operating earnings on a forward basis, a premium of less than 10% over its five-year average. It also trades at a premium over the S&P 500’s 22x forward earnings multiple, which could suggest some form of caution is necessary. Not just that, the lack of stock buybacks in Q4 is indicative of Buffett’s belief that even its stock may not be that attractive now when considering its valuation. Compounded with the possibly uncertain leadership transition risks to Vice Chair Greg Abel (although he has Buffett’s full confidence), I believe there are sufficient reasons for us to be careful in thinking about adding Berkshire stock aggressively here.

Furthermore, I think it’s increasingly clear that BRK.B stock hasn’t managed to overcome the $485 resistance zone since September last year, although support is assessed to be robust just over the $435 level. In other words, while no bearish signals is ascertained in Berkshire stock’s price chart, I believe the lack of follow on momentum over the past six months suggest we should buy closer to the $435 level, while avoiding adding until it manages to break above the $485 level and establish a new consolidation zone.

Berkshire remains a fundamentally strong stock and Buffett’s assurance of keeping his faith in equities should assure us that any talk about approaching a structural bearish turning point in the market is premature and possibly overstated. Yet, caution about adding more BRK.B now seems to be apt, as evinced by Buffett’s reticence in buying back more Berkshire stock in Q4. Hence, a Hold rating for now is likely the right move, while investors await another more attractive opportunity to add more positions.

Rating: Downgrade to Hold.
